Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic this semester. I'd try to carve out time between my part-time job and other responsibilities, but it rarely worked out the way I planned. I spent about 27 hours a week studying, but it was scattered—sometimes late nights, sometimes early mornings before work. I learn best by listening, so I'd often play lecture recordings while commuting or doing chores, which helped me feel like I was making progress even when I couldn't sit down properly.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, really driven by whatever crisis was most urgent. If a deadline was coming up, I'd cram hard for a few days, then barely touch the material afterward. With my attendance being so low—I missed a lot of classes due to work conflicts—I had to play catch-up constantly. Regular study times were a luxury I couldn't afford most weeks.
Q3: Usually it was panic that got me to log in—like realizing an assignment was due or that I'd missed something important. Sometimes I'd get a notification about a discussion post or a new resource being uploaded. Other times, I'd log in just to check if there was anything I'd forgotten about, which happened more often than I'd like to admit.
Q4: There were a few weeks early on where I tried to make it a habit, logging in every morning to check announcements. But then life got hectic—extra shifts at work, family stuff—and that routine fell apart. It never really became automatic for me. The LMS felt more like something I visited when I had to rather than something woven into my daily life.
Q5: The video lectures were a lifesaver for me since I'm an auditory learner. I watched over four hours worth because I could listen to them multiple times and really absorb the concepts. The discussion forums were also helpful when people asked questions I hadn't thought of—reading those exchanges sometimes clarified things better than the lectures themselves. Anything I could listen to or engage with through conversation felt most natural to me.
Q6: I barely touched the reading materials and PDFs, to be honest. With limited resources, I couldn't always print things out, and reading on a screen for hours just drained me. I also ignored a lot of the optional practice quizzes because they felt like extra work when I was already drowning. If something didn't seem immediately necessary for passing, it got pushed to the bottom of my list.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to the exam or assignments. If I could tell it would help me understand something I was struggling with, I'd prioritize it. Also, if it was something I could listen to or discuss with peers, that made it feel more valuable. Anything that felt like busywork or wasn't clearly tied to my grade got deprioritized fast.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a trap, honestly. I knew they might help, but when you're barely keeping your head above water, optional means skippable. Required activities had urgency—I had to do them or fail. Optional stuff always felt like something I'd get to 'eventually,' but eventually never came. There's a guilt that comes with skipping them, but survival mode doesn't leave room for extras.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of audio files and listened to them on my phone during my commute and at work when I could. I also used YouTube videos and free resources online to supplement what I was learning. My peer group was pretty active—we had a WhatsApp group where we'd discuss concepts and share notes. A lot of my real learning happened outside the LMS entirely.
Q10: There were many times I'd be on the bus with my earphones in, replaying a lecture I'd downloaded, or sitting with friends working through problems together. None of that shows up anywhere in the system. I'd also spend time just thinking through concepts while working my job—like mentally reviewing material during slow periods. The LMS only sees clicks, not the actual cognitive work happening.
Q11: Yeah, that was basically my strategy. I'd access a video or download a PDF once, then work with it offline for days. My internet at home isn't always reliable, so I learned to grab what I needed when I had good connectivity. The LMS would show one access, but I might have reviewed that material five or six times on my own.
Q12: Midway through the semester, everything just piled up. Work increased my hours, I was dealing with some family stress, and the course material got significantly harder. My motivation tanked even though I was trying to push through. The CBT sessions I attended helped me manage my anxiety a bit, but it was hard to feel engaged when I was running on empty.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about money, the last thing I wanted to do was log into the LMS and face more tasks. Confusion about the material made it worse—I'd avoid logging in because I didn't want to confront how behind I was. Competing responsibilities meant the LMS always lost the priority battle against immediate survival needs.
Q14: More times than I can count. I'd open a video and let it play while my mind wandered somewhere else entirely. Or I'd scroll through discussion posts without really processing anything. Sometimes I was just too tired to engage meaningfully, but I'd log in anyway hoping something would stick. It felt like going through the motions.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. The anxiety would build up, and instead of facing it, I'd avoid it. It's like my brain would rather deal with the stress of procrastination than the stress of actually doing the work and potentially failing. The CBT sessions helped me understand this pattern, but breaking it was another story.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to act. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly find the energy to log in and scramble. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have engaged at all some weeks. But this meant everything was last-minute and rushed, which hurt the quality of my work. The deadline was both my enemy and my only motivator.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it did affect me, but feedback was sparse and often came too late for me to adjust. Positive feedback gave me a temporary boost, made me feel like maybe I could do this. Negative feedback, or seeing my low scores, sometimes made me want to disengage entirely. It's hard to stay motivated when the numbers keep telling you you're failing.
Q18: The course structure assumed a level of consistency I couldn't maintain. Weekly modules made sense in theory, but when you miss a few weeks, catching up feels impossible. The structure didn't account for students like me who have unpredictable schedules. I needed more flexibility, more ways to engage that didn't require being present at specific times.
Q19: The heavy reliance on reading materials made things harder for me as an auditory learner. More audio content or podcasts would have helped enormously. The discussion forums were good, but they weren't always active when I had time to participate. What made it easier was when instructors posted video explanations—those I could actually absorb.
Q20: I think that describes me pretty well, actually. High activity might mean someone is trying, logging in, watching videos, participating in discussions—but if the assignments feel overwhelming or confusing, submission rates drop. You can be engaged with learning but still struggle to produce the tangible outputs the system wants. Effort doesn't always translate to completed work.
Q21: That's the opposite of my situation, but I can see how it happens. Some students are just efficient—they grab what they need quickly and study offline. Or they already have background knowledge and don't need to engage as much with the basics. The LMS rewards visible activity, but learning can happen in invisible ways.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you log in, what you click on, how long you spend. But they can't measure understanding, effort, or what's happening in your life outside the platform. They miss the offline studying, the group chats, the mental processing. They see behavior but not meaning. A log might show I watched a video, but not that I was crying from stress while doing it.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and making an effort to understand it, even when it's hard. It's not just clicking through materials—it's wrestling with concepts, asking questions, connecting ideas. For me, engagement also means showing up mentally, which is different from just showing up digitally. Some of my most engaged moments happened nowhere near a computer.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show that I was working 25 hours a week while taking a full course load. It wouldn't show the WhatsApp study sessions with classmates or the hours I spent listening to downloaded lectures on the bus. It wouldn't capture that I was dealing with high stress and moderate anxiety, or that I attended CBT sessions trying to get my head right. My engagement was real, even if the data doesn't reflect it.
